Global Data Centres and Electricity Consumption

Data centres for artificial intelligence and other forms of computing account for a small share of worldwide electricity consumption, according to estimates prepared by the International Energy Agency (IEA) and other researchers.

Consumption is growing very rapidly but from a low base and the extra demand will be dwarfed by increases from industrial users, electric vehicles, domestic appliances and airconditioning over the next decade.

From a global perspective, increased electricity demand from data centres is likely to have only a moderate impact on prices, service and reliability for other users.

But data centres are strongly clustered in a small number of countries, and within them a small number of regions, a pattern that is expected to persist.

In these regions, data centres account for a high and growing share of consumption, having a much greater impact on other users.

Data centres and artificial intelligence are expected to pose a relatively modest challenge for the global energy system in the future.

But managing their local impacts on availability, cost and reliability for other users is already proving a significant challenge ...

text:
RE: Hormuz offset flows

An error I still see people making months into tracking Hormuz oil flow is thinking you can add the entire ~7.5 MMbpd of combined Saudi Red Sea + UAE Fujairah flow as an offset to get back to that ~20 MMbpd pre-war Hormuz flow level.

But those offset routes didn't start at zero and were shipping ~3.5 MMbpd of oil prewar.

You get to add ~4 MMbpd to Hormuz flow from offsets, not 7+ MMbpd.

EIA weekly petroleum status: US crude inventories fell by 1.7 million barrels, while a further 3 million barrels were released from the Strategic Petroleum Reserve (SPR), taking the cumulative post-war drawdown to almost 100 million barrels. Gasoline stocks declined by 1.5 million barrels, with Gulf Coast inventories falling to their lowest level since 2017, highlighting continued regional tightness during the peak summer driving season. Distillate inventories, however, rose by 4.6 million barrels, providing some welcome relief to the segment of the refined products market that has experienced the greatest supply stress in recent months. 
Elsewhere, inventories at the Cushing delivery hub climbed back above 20 million barrels. US crude exports increased to 3.7 million bpd but remained below the one-year average of 4.2 million bpd and well below the record 6.4 million bpd reached in May. 
Domestic crude production was unchanged, while four-week average implied demand for both gasoline and distillates eased, broadly in line with normal seasonal trends.

Chicago and especially Paris #wheat futures have surged by more than 10% over the past week, lifting the September Paris milling wheat contract to a 17-month high above EUR 228 per tonne, a 5.5% gain today alone. The rally reflects a combination of acute geopolitical disruption and an increasingly tight global supply outlook.

The immediate catalyst was a sharp escalation in military attacks around the Sea of Azov, with strikes targeting Russian and Ukrainian grain export infrastructure. Temporary disruptions to shipping through the Don-Azov Channel and renewed attacks on Black Sea export ports raised concerns about exports from a region that accounts for a significant share of global wheat trade.

However, the market was already on edge. Severe drought in the US and Canada, deteriorating crop prospects across Europe, and the USDA's latest downward revisions to production and global ending stocks had already tightened the supply outlook. At the same time, poor European corn crops are expected to boost feed wheat demand, further increasing pressure on available supplies.

In short, weather-related production risks created a highly sensitive market, while the renewed disruption to Black Sea grain logistics provided the spark that triggered the latest leg higher in wheat prices.

A reminder that the real stress in energy markets is not in crude oil but in refined products. While Brent has rallied back above USD 80, diesel, gasoil and jet fuel continue to command exceptionally strong premiums as low inventories, refinery disruptions and peak seasonal demand tighten product availability.

As the charts highlight, crack spreads across the US, Europe and Asia have surged to multi-year highs, signalling that refiners - not crude producers - are currently capturing the bulk of the value. Refining margins are now approaching historic levels, creating a major windfall for operators at a time when global middle-distillate inventories remain critically low and supply chains continue to face disruption. Until refined product stocks begin to rebuild or demand eases seasonally, these elevated margins are likely to keep the energy complex well supported, even if crude supply itself remains relatively comfortable.

The bank believes that Apple's brand loyalty, supply chain bargaining power, and supply pressure faced by competitors make it easier to pass costs on to consumers.

Just in: Amazing conclusion! JPMorgan: The most pressing issue in the energy market is no longer Hormuz, but Russia.

It is undeniable that as the United States and Iran are once again at war with each other, the situation in the Strait of Hormuz is still the focus of global market attention.

However, as the crack spread, which reflects the difference between the price of crude oil and the price of its refined products, has widened unprecedentedly in recent weeks, some industry professionals are worried that a deeper problem in the energy market may be being overlooked by investors...

As Natasha Kaneva, head of commodity research at JPMorgan Chase, pointed out in the latest "Crude Oil Market Weekly Report", a new round of mutual attacks between the United States and Iran has caused another sharp drop in tanker traffic in the Strait of Hormuz, basically reversing the hard-won recovery momentum in the past few weeks.

However, after months of frequent disruptions, the market has actually become more relaxed when it comes to crude oil supply and tends to view it as a dynamic issue: Crude oil supplies can evaporate instantly and return just as quickly. However, the uncertainty in the refining and chemical sector is much greater right now.

As Kaneva writes, the question is no longer whether crude supplies can be restored, but how quickly the global refining system can process them.

In this regard, there are three main uncertainties: The extent of damage to the Middle East's refining infrastructure after months of conflict; the time it will take for China's refining capacity to be restored; and the speed of Russia's refining system to recover from repeated Ukrainian drone attacks.

On the Middle East front, JPMorgan has tracked more than 30 attacks on various refineries and oil processing infrastructure in the region, but the extent of the actual physical damage remains largely unknown.

The key question is what proportion of the region's 11.7 million barrels per day of refining capacity can be restarted immediately, and what proportion requires extensive maintenance.

In this regard, JPMorgan's baseline forecast is that by the end of this year, only 250,000 barrels per day of production capacity will remain closed, although the bank's confidence in this forecast is relatively low. At the same time, JPMorgan Chase believes that the most underestimated source of uncertainty currently is Russia.

Russia's refining capacity plummets Russia's refining system has been enduring repeated Ukrainian drone attacks over the past three months, which have damaged refineries, storage facilities and, increasingly, the complex secondary conversion units that determine product yields.

Russia's refinery throughput has fallen to 3.8 million barrels per day in June, 1.5 million barrels per day below the level at the beginning of the year, making Russia one of the biggest reasons for the unusually weak global refining activity today.

Data show that global oil refining and processing volume decreased by 8.2 million barrels per day this year, of which Russia alone accounted for about 20%. Domestically, Russia’s fuel supply situation has deteriorated significantly and is increasingly becoming an operational issue, not just a consumer one.

Shortages have spread even to regions with their own refineries as supplies are redirected to Moscow. This reflects a clear shift in targeting in Ukraine: in 2026, refineries supplying oil to Moscow accounted for 39% of all targets, compared with only 22% in 2025.

Fuel shortages are becoming increasingly acute as Russia enters its traditional "dacha" season - when millions of Russians head to their summer villas and seasonal gasoline demand reaches its annual peak. In some areas, drivers waited in line for days, fuel sales were rationed, and gas stations even temporarily ran out of fuel.

Retail prices have risen sharply, with some independent service stations reportedly selling 50% or more above normal levels. The impact of these supply disruptions now extends far beyond private car owners.

Agriculture, public transportation, utilities, logistics and small businesses are being increasingly impacted, signaling a shift from consumer inconvenience to widespread operational disruption across the economy.

Diesel supply issues are even more concerning because agricultural harvesting in Russia's southern regions typically begins in August, when demand for seasonal distillates typically reaches its peak of the year.

In the face of the crisis, the Russian government's current response logic is more focused on ensuring short-term fuel supply security than on substantive repairs of refining capacity.

At present, Russia has begun emergency imports of gasoline from Belarus and India, approved tax amendments aimed at stimulating gasoline production by increasing blending components, and specially approved refineries to produce and sell lower-standard Euro-3 gasoline to replace Euro-5, thereby increasing supply through low-standard blending materials.

As refinery operating rates decline and seasonal demand peaks resonate, Russia has also comprehensively tightened export controls on gasoline, aviation kerosene and diesel.

Xiaomo pointed out that although these measures are pragmatic measures to ensure domestic supply, they also reveal that Russia is currently only able to compensate for fuel shortages at the margin, rather than relying on a fully resilient oil refining system to operate. Will the impact be felt around the world?

JPMorgan predicts the developments could have repercussions far beyond Russia. Unable to process crude oil domestically, Russia will divert more crude oil to the export market, pushing crude oil exports closer to historical highs and exacerbating an already oversupplied crude oil market.

At the same time, the country's refined oil exports will decline sharply. The most affected are middle distillates. Russia's diesel and fuel oil exports fell by about two-thirds, further exacerbating a supply-demand imbalance in an already tight global distillate market and keeping refining margins high.

The reason why this is important is that Russia plays an extremely significant role in the global refined oil trade - it is the world's second largest diesel exporter, supplying approximately 12% of global diesel exports; it is also the largest exporter of fuel oil, with a market share of approximately 16%.

Therefore, disruptions in the Russian refining industry tend to have a far greater impact on the supply and demand balance of refined oil products than on the supply and demand balance of crude oil.

The key question is whether Russia's refining system will eventually return to something close to normal operations or whether it will slip into a less structural equilibrium punctuated by outages.

Before the escalation of drone attacks in early April this year, the normal processing capacity of Russian refineries remained at around 5.3 million barrels per day.

Based on this processing capacity, the system can produce approximately 2 million barrels of diesel, 1 million barrels of gasoline, 900,000 barrels of fuel oil, 300,000 barrels of aviation kerosene and 1.1 million barrels of other refined oil products per day.

Among them, the Russian domestic market consumes most of the gasoline and jet fuel, while nearly half of the diesel and almost all the fuel oil are exported, making it one of the core supply sources of the global distillate market.

Xiaomo pointed out that this normalized operating benchmark is the most effective reference for assessing the depth of current supply disruptions. Currently, the biggest risk to the market is the "persistence" of this shock.

Since late March, Ukraine’s offensive has evolved from sporadic “nuisance attacks” into a premeditated, long-term strategic operation aimed at paralyzing Russia’s entire refining and distribution system.

The frequent attacks, deeper domestic penetration, and wider attack scope all imply that refinery shutdowns are gradually becoming a structural norm in the market, rather than a fleeting temporary disturbance. In addition, Ukraine clearly knows how to hit Russia's "soft underbelly".

Recent attacks are increasingly focused on hydrocracking units, catalytic cracking units, reforming units and other secondary conversion units, and high-frequency "second strikes" are carried out on damaged facilities.

Compared with repairing relatively simple atmospheric distillation units, rebuilding these precision devices is extremely difficult and may directly extend the downtime period from just a few weeks to several quarters. As a result, the refined oil market is tighter than the crude oil market and is very likely to continue until 2027.

JPMorgan's baseline forecast favors a gradual recovery rather than a dramatic and rapid normalization. The bank predicts that the operating rate of Russian refineries will gradually rise from the current 3.6 million barrels per day to about 4.5 million barrels per day in early 2027, with the annual average in 2027 being about 4.7 million barrels per day.

The premise of this prediction is that the intensity of drone attacks will slow down as Ukraine shifts its defense focus back to its own energy infrastructure before the end of Russia's State Duma elections in September and the arrival of the winter heating season.

Even under this scenario, upstream crude oil production is expected to rebound only slightly, as downstream bottlenecks are increasingly affecting upstream investment decisions.

Russia's crude oil production currently remains at 8.7 million barrels per day, a decrease of nearly 600,000 barrels per day from the beginning of the year, which is approaching the actual delivery limit of its crude oil export infrastructure. If refinery utilization cannot be effectively boosted, excess crude oil will have nowhere to put it.

In other words, even though the global market is still naturally focused on the Strait of Hormuz, one of the most critical players in determining the global oil refining balance may have quietly moved to Russia, nearly 2,000 miles north of Hormuz. (

US June CPI crushed expectations – headline CPI +3.5% YoY (exp. 3.8%, prior 4.2%), -0.4% MoM (exp. -0.1%) – the first negative monthly print in six years. Core CPI +2.6% YoY (exp. 2.8%, prior 2.9%), 0.0% MoM.

3. 🏛️ Warsh Testimony: "Zero Tolerance" – Rejects "Mission Accomplished"

Chair Warsh delivered his first congressional testimony before the House Financial Services Committee, striking a firmly hawkish tone.

Key takeaways:

"Zero tolerance" for 5 years of high inflation – "This committee will not tolerate persistently elevated inflation"

Rejected "mission accomplished" – "Some see today's data and declare victory – I do not share that view"

Defended Fed independence – declined forward guidance, emphasized data-dependent decision-making

Launched major reforms – 5 task forces reviewing communication, balance sheet, data, inflation framework

AI as an inflation risk – AI investment is pushing up certain prices, adding a new dimension to inflation pressures

💬Warsh's debut sent one clear signal – the Fed won't be held hostage by one CPI print. Markets wanted a "rate pause" confirmation – they got "inflation surge will eventually become history" instead. He testifies before the Senate Banking Committee Wednesday – every word will be scrutinized.

USD/JPY at 162.2190, -0.15%.

📆 全球市场速览 · 2026年7月15日

🔥 超级星期二尘埃落定：CPI降温、沃什鹰派、油价高烧

昨晚市场迎来三重考验——美国6月CPI数据、美联储主席沃什国会首秀、五大行财报同时登场。结果是：CPI超预期降温，7月加息概率从42%骤降至17%；沃什展现强硬鹰派立场，直言对高通胀“零容忍”；油价在霍尔木兹海峡冲突中继续攀升，布伦特逼近85美元。三大力量相互抵消，美股最终小幅收涨。

1. 📈 美股：CPI降温点燃芯片股反弹，IBM暴跌25%拖累道指

美股三大指数全线收涨——道指微涨0.02%报52508.66点，标普500涨0.38%报7543.89点，纳指涨0.9%报26107.01点。道指大幅低开452点后迅速转升。

芯片股全面反攻：SK海力士ADR暴涨27.2%，美光科技涨近5%，英伟达涨超4%。银行股表现强劲：高盛Q2盈利增84%、收入创新高，股价大涨9%；摩根大通Q2多赚41%，美国银行多赚27%。

但IBM创下历史级暴跌：Q2初步业绩收入及每股盈利均令市场失望，股价急泻25%，录得至少58年以来最大单日跌幅，市值单日蒸发690亿美元。

大型科技股涨跌不一：谷歌涨近2%，Meta、特斯拉、亚马逊小幅收涨；SpaceX跌超2%，微软跌超1%，苹果跌0.77%。

💬 CPI降温给了市场“美联储暂停加息”的理由，芯片股和银行股借此反弹。但IBM的暴跌提醒我们——不是所有公司都在AI浪潮中受益。沃什的鹰派言论则为市场埋下伏笔：别把这份CPI当成“任务完成”的信号。

2. 🏛️ CPI：六年来首次环比负增长，7月加息概率骤降至17%

美国6月CPI数据全面低于预期——整体CPI同比涨3.5%（预期3.8%，前值4.2%），环比下滑0.4%（预期-0.1%），为六年来首次环比负增长。核心CPI同比升2.6%（预期2.8%，前值2.9%），环比录得0%。

能源价格是主要推手——6月能源指数环比下降5.7%，为2020年4月以来最大单月降幅，汽油价格指数环比下降9.7%。

数据公布后，7月加息概率从42%骤降至17%。两年期美债收益率一度下跌14个基点，10年期美债收益率收报4.591%，跌2.61个基点。

💬 这是一份足以改变美联储政策路径的通胀报告。但沃什在听证会上明确警告：“有些人看到今早的数据，就宣称任务完成、万事大吉。我绝不认同这种看法。”油价一周暴涨22%的效应尚未反映在6月数据中——7月的通胀才是真正的考验。

3. 🏛️ 沃什国会首秀：对高通胀“零容忍”，驳斥“任务完成论”

周二，美联储主席沃什首次以主席身份出席众议院金融服务委员会听证会。他在长达三小时的听证中展现出强硬的“鹰派”立场。

核心要点：

对持续五年的高通胀“零容忍” ——“委员会全体成员绝不容忍通胀长期居高不下”

Wind Financial Morning Post: July 16, 2026

Market Brief

China's GDP grew 4.7% year-over-year in the first half of the year.

RMB loans increased by 10.72 trillion yuan in the first half of the year, with social financing scale increment at 20.84 trillion yuan.

The number of cities in China's 70-big-city index reporting month-over-month price increases in new residential properties rose in June.

Changxin Technology begins subscription today, with a subscription cost of 4,330 yuan per lot.

Trump appears inclined to expand military action against Iran; Iran has no plans for negotiations.

Fed Chair Warsh: AI is pushing up prices but not necessarily inflation; no inflation metric is perfectly precise.

Global equity markets diverged on Wednesday, with storage chip stocks under selling pressure.

Mainland China's Apple AI features completed filing, with partners including Alibaba's Qwen.

Top Stories

China released its semi-annual economic report. GDP for the first half of the year reached 69.57 trillion yuan, up 4.7% year-over-year, with 5% growth in the first quarter and 4.3% in the second. Total retail sales of consumer goods and services rose 2.7%, value-added in the services sector increased 5.2%, and industrial output above designated size grew 5.4%. Fixed asset investment declined 5.7% year-over-year, with real estate development investment down 18% and new home sales area down 11.6%. The unsold housing inventory at the end of June fell 0.9% year-over-year, marking the fourth consecutive month of decline.

Housing prices in 70 major cities were released for June. Twenty cities recorded month-over-month increases in new home prices, up four from May. New home prices in first-tier cities rose 0.1% month-over-month, while second-hand home prices rose 0.3%, both marking four consecutive months of gains. Prices in second- and third-tier cities either declined or remained flat. According to the Ministry of Housing and Urban-Rural Development's online contract data, total new and second-hand home transactions in the four first-tier cities and twelve regional central cities rose 21.7% year-over-year in June, with 207 cities nationwide posting year-over-year growth in combined transactions.

Changxin Technology held its IPO online roadshow on July 15, with Chairman Zhu Yiming and management joined by representatives of the sponsoring institutions to address investor concerns regarding industry cycles, earnings sustainability, R&D progress, industry competition, and corporate governance stability. Zhu Yiming stated that, against the backdrop of domestic localization, the company is poised to continuously expand its market share. Changxin Technology will begin its new share subscription on July 16, with the online subscription code "787825" and the securities code and offline subscription code "688825". The offering price is set at 8.66 yuan per share, with one lot consisting of 500 shares; for retail investors, a successful allotment requires a payment of 4,330 yuan.

President Trump convened an emergency meeting to discuss a large-scale offensive plan against Iran, centered on a "new plan for devastating strikes against Iran's strategic targets." Sources indicate Trump appears willing to escalate military action to inflict sufficient damage to compel Iran to open the Strait of Hormuz and accept his demands on nuclear issues. Deputy Foreign Minister Gharibabadi of Iran stated Iran has never left the negotiating table, accusing the U.S. of tearing up the memorandum of understanding and asserting Iran will never be the first to request negotiations.

The U.S. Central Command announced that at 3 p.m. Eastern Time on July 15 (3 a.m. Beijing time on July 16), U.S. forces carried out a second wave of strikes against Iran, targeting military facilities threatening freedom of navigation through the Strait of Hormuz.

Fed Chair Kevin Warsh, in his first congressional testimony since taking office, addressed multiple market-sensitive topics over two days. Warsh acknowledged improvements in June's CPI and PPI data but noted that inflation metrics cannot perfectly reflect underlying inflation pressures. He declared a "zero tolerance" stance toward inflation and reaffirmed a firm commitment to restoring price stability. While not explicitly signaling a policy tightening, he indicated interest rate adjustments remain an option to curb inflation. Warsh emphasized his independence, stating President Trump did not attempt to influence monetary policy. He argued that price increases driven by AI infrastructure investment do not necessarily fuel inflation, and expects AI to boost productivity and wages.

China Macro & Policy

President Xi Jinping, while inspecting Shanghai, stressed that high-quality urban renewal is a critical driver of urban modernization. He called for full implementation of the people-centered urban philosophy, emphasizing public input in planning, execution, and evaluation to enhance residents’ sense of gain, happiness, and security.

The People's Bank of China reported that RMB deposits increased by 17.76 trillion yuan in the first half of the year, RMB loans rose 10.72 trillion yuan, and bond financing added 8.51 trillion yuan. The cumulative social financing scale increment reached 20.84 trillion yuan, down 2.02 trillion yuan year-over-year. At the end of June, M2 balance grew 8% year-over-year.

The State Council Information Office held a press briefing on monetary policy implementation and financial data for the first half of the year. PBOC Deputy Governor Zou Lan said the central bank will continue to steadily advance reforms to its monetary policy framework to better guide market overnight rates to remain near policy rates. It will study increasing the frequency of overnight reverse repo operations in line with primary dealer demand and improve market communication. Xie Guangqi, Director of the PBOC's Monetary Policy Department, noted that over the coming period, monetary and credit growth will shift from extensive expansion to intensive development, with "slower but higher-quality" loan growth likely becoming a new norm.

The National Bureau of Statistics reported that the average urban survey unemployment rate for the first half of the year was 5.2%. In June, the rate fell 0.1 percentage point to 5.0%. Per capita disposable income nationwide rose 5.2% nominally and 4.2% in real terms year-over-year to 22,981 yuan.

New U.S. legislation targeting Russia will impose sanctions on five major buyers of Russian crude oil and natural gas, including China. The bill authorizes the Trump administration to impose tariffs of up to 100% on these countries. Foreign Ministry spokesperson Lin Jian responded that applying double standards and coercive pressure will ultimately backfire.

China Equities Markets

A-shares traded flat amid weakness. The SSE Index fell 0.29% to 3,955.58, the Shenzhen Component Index dropped 0.97%, and the ChiNext Index declined 1.21%. The Wind All China Index fell 0.75%, with market turnover at 2.59 trillion yuan. Consumer spending rebounded broadly, with pharmaceuticals and alcohol leading gains. Semiconductor and AI hardware supply chain stocks retreated, with storage chip and PCB concepts sharply lower.

Hong Kong markets advanced. The Hang Seng Index rose 1.4% to 24,681.10, the Hang Seng Tech Index gained 1.3%, and the Hang Seng China Enterprises Index climbed 1%. Biotech stocks surged, and large model-related stocks rose strongly. Semiconductor and non-ferrous metal sectors underperformed. Southbound funds net bought RMB 13.364 billion, with Alibaba, Tencent, ZhiPu, and Meituan seeing significant inflows.

Amid market volatility, capital continues to flow into ETFs. Wind data show that on July 14, multiple ETFs recorded net inflows, with semiconductor and AI-themed ETFs attracting the most attention. Broad-market ETFs including CSI 300, CSI 500, and CSI 1000 also drew substantial inflows. Over the past week, technology-themed and broad-market ETFs remained the primary focus of capital allocation.

As of July 15, 1,692 A-share companies disclosed their 2026 first-half earnings forecasts, with 731 posting positive outlooks, a 43.2% ratio. Excluding turnaround cases, Jiangbo Long, Yisheng Shares, Tianqi Lithium, Beidou Star, Xianglu Tungsten, Hengyi Petrochemical, Estun, Shannon Semiconductor, Yidao Information, and Sanwei Communications rank among the top ten forecasted profit growers. Jiangbo Long is projected to report attributable net profit of 9.2 billion to 11 billion yuan in the first half of 2026, up 62,204% to 74,394% year-over-year, ranking first.

China Industry News

The Cyberspace Administration of China disclosed filing information for seven mobile AI models, covering Apple, Huawei, Xiaomi, OPPO, vivo, Samsung, and ZTE. Notably, Alibaba's Qwen will be integrated into Apple's AI, and Baidu will provide search functionality for Apple's AI. Industry insiders expect multiple smartphone brands to launch AI-powered phones this September.

Hugging Face reported that Chinese open-source models account for 41% of downloads on its platform, surpassing U.S. models. On the AI model calling platform OpenRouter, the top six most popular models are all from Chinese institutions, including Tencent, Xiaomi, DeepSeek, MiniMax, and ZhiPu.

The "Automotive Industry Whole Vehicle Cost Estimation Rules" group standard was officially released. This is China's first unified cost estimation framework for automotive manufacturing, ending the industry's long-standing problem of inconsistent cost accounting and lack of benchmarking.

The National Energy Administration reported that total electricity consumption nationwide reached 5,099.9 billion kilowatt-hours in the first half of the year, up 5.3% year-over-year. Charging and battery swapping services and internet data services posted the fastest growth, at 56.9% and 44%, respectively.

The Semiconductor Industry Association (SEMI) forecast global semiconductor manufacturing equipment sales to reach a record $165.9 billion this year, up 23.2%. Global equipment sales are expected to grow for a fifth consecutive year, reaching $229.5 billion by 2028.

The first prescription for the implanted brain-computer interface system for hand motor function compensation (NEO) was issued in Shanghai, following the world's first implantation at Huashan Hospital, Fudan University. Intraoperative tests showed stable, high-quality epidural EEG signals. The patient's vital signs remain stable post-surgery.

Meituan Bike, Didi Qingjue, and Hello Bike recently adjusted pricing rules in Beijing and other cities, implementing a combined strategy of raising base prices and extending free riding duration. Base rates rose from 1.5 yuan per 30 minutes to 1.88–1.99 yuan per 60 minutes.

Fintech

Leading banks and large model firms are launching AI computing rights "product competitions." Since June, China Merchants Bank, Ping An Bank, MYbank, Shanghai Pudong Development Bank, Agricultural Bank of China, and Bank of Communications have introduced AI debit or credit cards offering benefits such as token grants, AI membership services, intelligent agent tools, and professional database access.

Japan's parliament approved revisions to the Financial Instruments and Exchange Act and the Payment Services Act, reclassifying cryptocurrencies from primarily payment instruments to investment assets alongside other financial products, introducing stricter insider trading rules and disclosure requirements. The new rules are expected to take effect in 2027.

Corporate News & Updates

In response to reports suggesting manufacturing issues may delay Vera Rubin, NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang stated that Vera Rubin hardware is already in production and on track for customer delivery, moving toward "mass-scale" manufacturing.

Sources indicate that Dutch lithography maker ASML plans to raise equipment prices. The company has discussed increasing prices for its extreme ultraviolet (EUV) lithography systems with TSMC and intends to raise prices for deep ultraviolet (DUV) systems by 10%. However, TSMC is resisting ASML's efforts to raise prices on any equipment category.

According to KeyBanc and FactSet reports, Intel's next-generation process technologies, including 18A and 14A, have achieved significant success, with 18A yield rising from 65% to 85%. Intel's foundry business has partnered with AMD, NVIDIA, Marvell, Microsoft, Micron, and OpenAI.

According to industry sources, Samsung Electronics plans to build a new DRAM manufacturing facility at its Giheung semiconductor complex, targeting a monthly capacity of 100,000 wafers, with total investment reaching tens of trillions of won. Due to labor shortages, Samsung is considering outsourcing backend design work for Google's 2nm TPU I/O chips.

Nokia and NVIDIA unveiled the industry's first commercial AI-RAN platform, capable of significantly boosting data transmission capacity without expanding existing infrastructure. The platform will begin pilot deployments by year-end and enter commercial use in 2027.

Wafer foundry PSMC announced it will raise storage foundry pricing by 45% starting July, with logic foundry pricing for 8-inch and 12-inch wafers also increasing 10–15%. The company expects the DRAM supply-demand gap to persist until 2027.

Financial technology company Stripe and private equity firm Advent are reportedly exploring a joint acquisition of legacy online payment firm PayPal. The proposed offer is $60.50 per share, valuing the company at over $53 billion.

Global Macro & Policy

U.S. President Trump stated that maintaining current interest rates is preferable to raising them and expressed a desire to see rates decline. He said he deeply respects Fed Chair Kevin Warsh.

President Trump claimed Russia is ready to quickly reach an agreement to end the Ukraine conflict and believes the war will conclude during his term.

Media reports indicate the White House is considering an executive order to restrict open-source AI. Discussions remain in early stages, and it is unclear whether they will lead to concrete policy.

U.S. June PPI rose 5.5% year-over-year, significantly below the 6.2% forecast; core PPI rose 4.7%, also below the 5.2% forecast. A sharp drop in oil prices was the main reason for the cooler-than-expected data, though upstream cost pressures remain elevated, with prices for processed goods, raw materials, and metals still rising significantly.

The Fed's Beige Book showed overall prices rose modestly, with nine districts reporting modest increases, two reporting significant increases, and one reporting slight gains. Compared with the prior reporting period, price increases in all districts were flat or slowed. Businesses generally expect continued economic expansion in the coming months, though several districts noted high uncertainty regarding fuel cost outlooks.

New York Fed President Williams said that although AI-driven demand poses upward pressure on inflation, the current monetary policy stance remains "appropriate" to bring inflation back to the 2% target. He expressed encouraging signs that inflation has peaked and should gradually decline over the next few quarters.

The Bank of Canada held its benchmark rate steady at 2.25%, marking its sixth consecutive pause, in line with market expectations. The central bank noted that, excluding gasoline prices, overall inflation remains near the 2% target.

The UK government announced a "curfew" on social media use for 16- and 17-year-olds. This follows its June announcement banning social media apps for under-16s.

Global Equity Markets

U.S. major indexes closed slightly higher. The Dow Jones Industrial Average rose 0.29% to 52,658.64, the S&P 500 gained 0.38% to 7,572.40, and the Nasdaq Composite advanced 0.62% to 26,269.23. Apple rose over 4%, and Google rose more than 3%, leading the Dow. The Wind US Magnificent Seven Index climbed 2.47%, with Meta and Amazon up over 3%. SpaceX fell 0.6%. The Nasdaq Golden Dragon China Index rose 2.92%, with Miniso up over 9% and Manbang Group up over 6%. Storage chip stocks plunged, with SK Hynix down 9%, Micron, SanDisk, and Western Digital down over 8%.

European major indexes closed mixed. Germany's DAX fell 0.59% to 24,999.53; France's CAC 40 rose 0.19% to 8,382.43; UK's FTSE 100 fell 0.13% to 10,515.92. Geopolitical tensions continued to suppress risk appetite; the escalation of U.S.-Iran conflict pushed energy prices higher, and ECB officials maintained a cautious stance on inflation, with market sentiment overall defensive.

The EIA has been overestimating US crude inventories by about 13.5 million barrels over the last three weeks. You can see it in the recent spike in the modified adjustment on the chart.

These adjustments often reverse lower later, which means the actual inventory picture could end up tighter than the initial reports suggested. It’s a reminder that weekly EIA numbers can be noisy and subject to revisions.

For the energy side, a tighter actual supply backdrop is generally supportive over time, but these are short-term data points. The bigger picture still comes down to your own process, risk management, and how you weigh the fundamentals versus the weekly noise.

The United Arab Emirates reported to OPEC that its crude output jumped by 80% in June 2026 from the previous month, reaching 3.81 million barrels per day, an increase of 1.64 million barrels a day from May. The surge came after the UAE formally exited OPEC at the beginning of May to escape production quotas and bring oil to market on its own terms. Before the war, UAE output stood at 3.42 million barrels a day in February, and the International Energy Agency said the country’s production hit a record monthly high of 4.1 million bpd in June. The UAE had long resisted OPEC+ quotas because its production capacity had grown to 4.8 million bpd while it was only allowed to pump 3.2 million bpd under the group’s framework. ADNOC plans to expand that capacity to 5 million bpd by 2027. 

The output increase occurred even as tensions between the U.S. and Iran curtailed traffic through the Strait of Hormuz, a critical export route that normally carries about a fifth of the world’s oil. Leaving OPEC gave Abu Dhabi greater flexibility to expand production and invest in infrastructure aimed at reducing reliance on Hormuz, including using the Fujairah terminal on the Gulf of Oman to export 1.7 million bpd of crude and refined fuels. The IEA expects UAE output to reach 5.2 million bpd in 2027, making it one of the largest contributors to non-OPEC+ supply growth. The UAE’s exit on May 1 marked the biggest structural shake-up for OPEC+ in years, and seven remaining members agreed to raise collective production by 188,000 bpd in June as they unwind voluntary cuts. Analysts called the UAE departure a big deal for OPEC, noting the country had been producing close to 30% below its capacity of 4.85 million bpd under the group’s rules.
